LAW AND MOTION 302, OCT-10-2023. HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR LEAVE FOR PERMISSION TO FILE AN OVERSIZED OPPOSITION TO DEFENDANT MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. RULING IS ARGUED. AT CONCLUSION OF ARGUMENT, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. PRO PER PLAINTIFF'S "MOTION FOR LEAVE FOR PERMISSION TO FILE AN OVERSIZED OPPOSITION TO DEFENDANT MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT" IS DENIED. FIRST, PLAINTIFF'S REGULAR NOTICED MOTION IS UNTIMELY; IT WAS FILED 11 COURT DAYS (SEPTEMBER 25) BEFORE ITS HEARING DATE (OCTOBER 10), WHEN A MINIMUM OF 16 COURT DAYS IS REQUIRED. (CCP 1005(B).) SECOND, PLAINTIFF'S 40-PLUS-PAGE OPPOSITION HAS ALREADY BEEN FILED, BUT AN APPLICATION FOR AN OVERSIZED MEMORANDUM MUST BE FILED "BEFORE THE MEMORANDUM IS DUE." (CRC 3.1113(E).) THIRD, GIVEN THAT DEFENDANT'S SUMMARY JUDGMENT MEMORANDUM IS 20 PAGES LONG, THE COURT RULES THAT PLAINTIFF SHALL FILE A PAGE-COMPLAINT OPPOSITION MEMORANDUM, AND HEREBY SO ORDERS. (CRC 3.1113(D).) THE COURT NOTES AN ERROR IN THE COURT'S TENTATIVE RULING AND ADVISES THE PARTIES THAT IT INTENDS TO UPDATE THE PROPOSED ORDER ACCORDINGLY. JUDGE: RICHARD B. ULMER JR. CLERK: ROBERT WOODS. COURT REPORTER: CESAR RODRIHUEZ, CSR: 13269, T: (909) 210-2171, E: CSRODRIGUEZ1@MSN.COM. (302/RBU) ***AFTER HEARING, AN ORDER IS SIGNED.

Administrative Motion To Continue Summary Of Judgment
Off Calendar Sep-29-2023 Advanced From Oct-13-23 To Oct-06-23 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 302

Motion For Leave For Permission To File An Oversized Opposition To Defendant Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Oct-10-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom):

Ramin Dadashian, Plaintiff in propria persona.

Puja Sharma, Esq., LITTLER MENDELSON, P.C., (415) 433-1940, for Defendant.

RE: Plaintiff's Motion For Leave For Permission To File An Oversized Opposition To Defendant Motion For Summary Judgment.

Ruling is argued. At conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling. Pro per plaintiff's "motion for leave for permission to file an oversized opposition to defendant motion for summary judgment" is denied. First, plaintiff's regular noticed motion is untimely; it was filed 11 court days (September 25) before its hearing date (October 10), when a minimum of 16 court days is required. (CCP 1005(b).) Second, plaintiff's 40-plus-page opposition has already been filed, but an application for an oversized memorandum must be filed "before the memorandum is due." (CRC 3.1113(e).) Third, given that defendant's summary judgment memorandum is 20 pages long, the court rules that plaintiff shall file a page-complaint opposition memorandum, and hereby so orders. (CRC 3.1113(d).)

The court notes an error in the court's tentative ruling and advises the parties that it intends to update the proposed order accordingly.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr. Clerk: Robert Woods. Court Reporter: Cesar Rodrihuez, CSR: 13269, T: (909) 210-2171, E: csrodriguez1@msn.com.. (302/RBU)

***After hearing, an order is signed.

Administrative Motion To Continue Summary Of Judgment
Off Calendar Oct-06-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

RE: Plaintiff's Motion To Continue Summary Of Judgment.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling. DENIED. Plaintiff Dadashian seeks to continue the pending summary judgment motion by a year. This would be well after the trial date set for December 2023. Defendant' summary judgment motion was filed Augist 10, 2023. This type of motion has the longest notice period in California procedure: at least 75 days' notice, which is designed to allow the other side (Dadashian in this case) plenty of time to prepare an opposition. To obtain a continuance, Dadashian should have prepared an affidavit (or declaration under penalty of perjury, in accordance with CCP 2015.5) that shows "(1) the facts to be obtained are essential to opposing the motion; (2) there is reason to believe such facts may exist; and (3) the reasons why additional time is needed to obtain these facts." Wachs v. Curry, 13 Cal.App.4th 616, 623 (1993) (internal citations omitted). Simply informing the court that more work needs to done, including generally more discovery, is not sufficient. Bahl v. Bank of America, 89 Cal.App.4th 389, 397 (2001); Roth v. Rhodes, 25 Cal.App.4th 530, 548 (1994). It is also true that the Dadashian has to show that he was diligent in trying to get the specific evidence he now cites as a reason for delay. A&B Painting and Drywall, Inc. v. Superior Court, 25 Cal.App.4th 349, 356-357 (1994); Rodriguez v. Oto (2013) 212 CA4th 1020, 1038 & fn. 7 (noting that "majority of courts have held that lack of diligence may be a ground for denying a request for a continuance") (internal quotes omitted); Braganza v. Albertson's LLC (2021) 67 CA5th 144, 151-157. The current motion to delay the summary judgment motion, and the plaintiff's "Response" filed October 3, 2023, don't make of the showing required. I don't know what specific discovery plaintiff wants to accomplish, how it relates to the summary judgment motion, nor why he didn't accomplish the work after he got the summary judgment motion in August. Both under CCP 437c(h) and my inherent authority to grant continuances, First State Ins. Co. v. Superior Court (2000) 79 Cal.App.4th 324, 334, I deny the motion.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Curtis E.A. Karnow. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/CEK)
